insights: Employees with 0–3 months of employment tenure exhibit a 48.31% expense decline rate—more than triple the 13.24% and 18.99% rates for the 3–6 months and 6–12 months groups respectively—indicating a severe, statistically significant compliance gap among newest hires (p < 0.000001).

The elevated rejection pattern among 0–3 month employees is systematic across departments (observed in 5 of 7), time periods (consistent from 2023Q2 to 2024Q2), and not attributable to data anomalies or isolated units—confirming it reflects a foundational onboarding or policy comprehension issue rather than localized operational noise.

Travel-related expenses drive the highest tenure-sensitive risk: 0–3 month employees face a 59.0% decline rate for Travel claims versus just 15.8% for those with 3–6 months tenure, suggesting this category’s documentation or policy requirements are disproportionately challenging for new hires.
Assets is the second-highest risk expense category for new employees, reinforcing that high-complexity, asset-justification–dependent submissions compound early-tenure compliance vulnerability.

Negative tenure records (40% of all expense entries) reflect a pervasive data integrity issue—expenses logged before employee start dates—which undermines trust in system-recorded timelines and may indicate broader process gaps in onboarding synchronization or expense submission controls.

The 12+ months tenure group shows only 8 valid records (25% decline rate), limiting generalizability for long-tenure comparisons but confirming that the observed risk gradient is sharply concentrated in the first quarter of employment.
summary: New employees with 0–3 months of tenure are over three times more likely to have expenses declined (48.31%) compared to peers with 3–12 months tenure, and this pattern is consistent across departments, quarters, and expense types—confirming a systemic onboarding compliance gap rather than a departmental or temporal anomaly. Travel and Assets categories represent the highest-risk expense types for new hires, with Travel showing the steepest tenure-based decline differential (59.0% vs. 15.8%). The widespread presence of negative tenure records (40% of all entries) signals a critical data integrity weakness in how expense timing aligns with employment start dates, potentially exacerbating confusion during early-tenure policy learning. These findings collectively point to urgent needs in targeted onboarding training, simplified guidance for high-risk expense categories, and tighter integration between HR onboarding systems and the expense platform. Next focus should be validating and implementing role-specific, just-in-time policy prompts for Travel and Assets submissions during the first 90 days of employment.
